sections.heading: Modern contraceptive use rose a little, with more use of short-term methods
sections.body: Among all women, modern contraceptive use reached 25.7 percent in 2025, up from 24.3 percent in 2022. Pills were still the most common modern method at 11.6 percent. Injectables increased from 2.8 to 3.4 percent, and implants increased from 1.7 to 2.4 percent. Female sterilization slipped from 5.0 to 4.6 percent, so the small national rise came more from reversible methods than from permanent ones.

sections.heading: The highest-use regions were mostly in the south and island areas
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest modern contraceptive use among all women at 37.1 percent. It was followed by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. These regions were well above the national level of 25.7 percent, with each at least 6.5 points higher. Pills were also a major part of their method mix: Davao was at 18.6 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 17.1 percent, Caraga at 15.0 percent, and MIMAROPA at 14.4 percent.

sections.heading: Low modern use appeared in different kinds of regions
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest modern contraceptive use at 13.0 percent, but low use was not found in only one type of area. Central Visayas was also low at 21.0 percent, NCR at 21.4 percent, and Bicol Region at 22.1 percent. The lower-use group includes both a highly urban region and regions outside the metropolitan core, suggesting that regional differences are not simply an urban-rural story.

sections.heading: Some regions shifted a lot even though the national rate barely changed
sections.body: From 2022 to 2025, the national increase was only 1.4 points, but several regions changed much more. MIMAROPA rose from 26.8 to 33.9 percent, Davao Region from 32.3 to 37.1 percent, Eastern Visayas from 23.1 to 27.6 percent, and Central Luzon from 24.6 to 28.9 percent. In contrast, Central Visayas fell from 26.9 to 21.0 percent, Cagayan Valley from 34.3 to 28.7 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ from 28.5 to 24.7 percent.

sections.heading: Regions reached similar totals through different method mixes
sections.body: Pills were the main modern method in most regions, but regions with high or middle levels of use did not all look the same. MIMAROPA combined 14.4 percent pill use with the highest injectable level at 8.0 percent and the highest implant level at 5.8 percent. CAR stood out for female sterilization at 10.0 percent, while Northern Mindanao had the highest IUD level at 4.0 percent. These differences show that similar modern-use totals can hide very different method profiles.

sections.heading: Modern use was highest in the middle reproductive ages
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was highest among women aged 35 to 39 at 41.1 percent. The next highest groups were ages 40 to 44 at 38.4 percent and 30 to 34 at 38.0 percent. Use was much lower among women aged 15 to 19 at 2.5 percent and 20 to 24 at 15.4 percent, reflecting the all-women denominator and different life stages. The method mix also changes with age: pill use peaks around ages 30 to 39, while female sterilization becomes much more visible from age 35 onward.

sections.heading: Modern use was highest among women with three or four children
sections.body: Women with three or four living children had the highest modern contraceptive use at 55.8 percent. This was higher than 46.5 percent among women with five or more children and 37.9 percent among women with one or two children. Women with three or four living children also had the highest pill use at 22.6 percent and the highest female sterilization level at 15.9 percent. Women with no living children were much lower at 2.4 percent, which is expected in an all-women measure but still shows how strongly modern use is tied to family-building stage.

sections.heading: Rural women reported higher modern use than urban women
sections.body: In 2025, modern contraceptive use was 28.8 percent among rural women and 23.3 percent among urban women. The rural advantage was mainly in pills, at 14.2 percent compared with 9.6 percent in urban areas, and in implants, at 2.9 percent compared with 2.0 percent. Male condom use went the other way: 2.3 percent in urban areas and 1.3 percent in rural areas.

sections.heading: Modern use did not rise or fall neatly with education
sections.body: Modern use was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education at 40.1 percent and lowest among women with Grades 11-12 at 11.8 percent. College-educated women were at 21.7 percent, while women with post-secondary education were at 32.0 percent. This is not a simple education ladder, and the education table alone does not explain why these differences appear.

sections.heading: Modern use was lower in higher wealth groups
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was highest in the lowest wealth quintile at 32.5 percent and lowest in the highest quintile at 20.0 percent. Pills, injectables, and implants all followed this lower-to-higher wealth pattern. Male condom use was different, with the highest level in the highest quintile at 3.1 percent. This means the wealth pattern is not only about total modern use; it also shows which modern methods are most visible in each wealth group.

takeaways: Among all women, modern contraceptive use rose modestly to 25.7 percent in 2025, and pills still made up the largest share of modern use.
Regional differences were wide: Davao Region, MIMAROPA, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga were far above the national level, while BARMM remained much lower.
The strongest subgroup differences were by number of living children, wealth, education, and region. The urban-rural gap was smaller, but it still favored rural women.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
